assertions.expectation: Explains this is related to the interaction between maxSurge, drain order, and pod scheduling
assertions.reason: Response clearly explains the relationship between maxSurge settings, simultaneous draining of nodes, and pod scheduling causing the musical chairs effect

assertions.expectation: Notes that GKE cordons nodes before draining them — pods should prefer scheduling onto uncordoned (upgraded) nodes
assertions.reason: Response doesn't explicitly mention that GKE cordons nodes before draining or explain that pods should prefer uncordoned nodes

assertions.expectation: Identifies possible cause: too many nodes being drained simultaneously (high maxSurge) before enough upgraded nodes exist
assertions.reason: Response explicitly identifies high maxSurge relative to available upgraded nodes as the root cause and explains the simultaneous draining issue

assertions.expectation: Recommends reducing maxSurge to 1 to ensure upgraded nodes are available before draining the next batch
assertions.reason: Response provides specific command to set maxSurge to 1 and explains this ensures upgraded nodes are available before next batch drains

assertions.expectation: Suggests using pod anti-affinity or node affinity to prefer already-upgraded nodes
assertions.reason: Response provides node affinity YAML configuration to prefer nodes at target version/machine type

assertions.expectation: Mentions checking if any taints or tolerations are causing pods to land on the wrong nodes
assertions.reason: Response discusses taint-based approach and includes kubectl taint commands for nodes being drained

assertions.expectation: Provides kubectl commands to check node cordoned status and pod scheduling events
assertions.reason: Response provides multiple kubectl commands for monitoring pod movements, events, and node status during upgrades

assertions.expectation: Recommends setting maxUnavailable=0 to ensure old nodes aren't removed until pods are safely rescheduled
assertions.reason: Response includes --max-unavailable-upgrade 0 in the gcloud command to ensure old nodes aren't removed prematurely

assertions.expectation: May mention GKE's autoscaled blue-green upgrade strategy as an alternative that avoids this issue by cordoning the entire old pool before migrating workloads to new nodes
assertions.reason: Response dedicates a section to autoscaled blue-green strategy, explains how it cordons the old pool and creates separate green pool, completely eliminating musical chairs
